Appearances:
JOHN H. CIGAVIC III. Esq., (415) 859-5065 BASIC LEGAL SERVICES basiclegalservices.me@gmail.com Attorney for Dorothy Riedel
Trevor B. McCann, Esq., COLLINS + COLLINS LLP (510) 844-5100 tmccann@ccllp.law Attorney for DEFENDANT/CROSS-COMPLAINANT JOHN MUNGUIA

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument modifes it's tenative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JOHN MUNGUIA's Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Appointing Elisor [CCP 128(A)(4), 177].

Defendant's motion to appoint an elisor is granted and is to be effective on December 12, 2024.

The motion is timely. Defendant previously calendared this motion and, by minute order of November 12, 2024, the court expressly allowed defendant to re-calendar the motion on a date after November 28, 2024. In any event, because plaintiff addressed the merits, she waived any notice defect. (See Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697 [party may waive notice defect by addressing the merits].)

Per the inherent powers of the court, there is good cause to appoint an elisor to effectuate this court's prior orders (including the 9/12/24 assignment order) and judgment. (CCP 128(a)(8); CCP 177; Blueberry Properties, LLC v. Chow (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 1017, 1020 ["A court typically appoints an elisor to sign documents on behalf of a recalcitrant party in order to effectuate its judgments or orders, where the party refuses to execute such documents."].) Plaintiff has demonstrated a pattern of recalcitrance and stymied defendant's collection efforts.

The court does not entertain plaintiff's arguments regarding the propriety of the assignment order because: (1) many, if not all, of those arguments regarding exemptions and other matters were never previously raised and ostensibly waived (see 8/26/24 opposition to assignment); (2) re-visiting that order would be an improper motion for reconsideration; and (3) plaintiff has appealed the order and may raise those issues with the Court of Appeal.

The court denies plaintiff's request for a continuance due to the protracted history of this litigation.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
JOHN H. CIGAVIC III. Esq., (415) 859-5065 BASIC LEGAL SERVICES basiclegalservices.me@gmail.com Attorney for Dorothy Riedel
Trevor B. McCann, Esq., COLLINS + COLLINS LLP (510) 844-5100 tmccann@ccllp.law Attorney for DEFENDANT/CROSS-COMPLAINANT JOHN MUNGUIA

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JOHN MUNGUIA's Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys' Fees And Costs Incurred To Enforce Judgment [Welf. & Inst. Code 15657.5, Subd. (A); CCP 685.040, 685.070].

Defendant's motion for attorney's fees and costs is granted in large part. Defendant is awarded $68,292.25 in fees and costs.

The motion is timely. Defendant previously calendared this motion and, by minute order of November 12, 2024, the court expressly allowed defendant to re-calendar the motion on a date after November 28, 2024. In any event, because plaintiff addressed the merit, she waived any notice defect. (See Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697 [party may waive notice defect by addressing the merits].)

Defendant is entitled to fees for prevailing in this action and for its collection efforts. (See Welfare & Institutions Code section 15657.5(a); CCP 685.040; 685.070.)
The court employes the lodestar method to determine reasonable fees. (See Lealao v. Beneficial California Inc. (2000) 82 Cal.App.4th 19, 26 [number of hours times reasonable rate].) Counsel's billable hourly rate of $395/hour is reasonable. Defendant claims $72,637.25 and cites counsel's billing records. (McCann, Ex. 8.)
The court strikes $1580 from the requested sum because the reference to the Wong trial was an error (McCann, Ex. 8 [1/16/24 entry]). Defendant concedes the mistake. (Reply, 4:10-11.)

The court further strikes $2765 for defendant's ex parte request to appoint an elisor. Defendant should have known that the request to appoint an elisor would be contested and was not a proper ex parte application.
